title: How Eating Healthier Could Reshape Agriculture
gist: A new Nature study projects that global adoption of the EAT-Lancet Planetary Health Diet by 2050 would cut livestock production value by 70%, raise vegetable/fruit/legume production by 57%, and free up 6% of the world's farmland for other uses.

insights.claim: In 2025 the EAT Foundation and The Lancet formed the EAT-Lancet Commission, which published the Planetary Health Diet — a plant-forward diet strictly limiting meats, fats, and sugars, designed to be both healthier for people and better for the planet than the typical Western diet.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Establishes the reference diet framework now being used to model global agricultural transformation scenarios through 2050.
insights.locator: So in 2025 the EAT Foundation teamed up with The Lancet

insights.claim: A new study published in Nature models how global adoption of the EAT-Lancet Planetary Health Diet would transform agriculture by 2050, projecting a 70 percent drop in the production value of cattle, sheep, and goats and a 57 percent increase in vegetable, fruit, and legume production.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A large-scale dietary shift scenario implies a massive reallocation of agricultural capital away from livestock toward produce — relevant to long-horizon positioning in agriculture, land use, and protein-alternative sectors.
insights.locator: Now, a new study published in Nature looks at

insights.claim: Under the modeled global shift to the Planetary Health Diet, the resulting land-use changes would open up 6 percent of the world's farmland for other, more environmentally friendly activities.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A quantified upper-bound estimate of land freed by dietary transition — a variable relevant to carbon-offset, rewilding, and alternative land-use investment theses.
insights.locator: On net, these shifts in land use would open up

insights.claim: Western diets, heavy in fat, sugar, and red meat, are described as both unhealthy for people and environmentally costly, contributing to habitat loss and greenhouse gas emissions through their production.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Frames the health and climate cases for dietary change as aligned, strengthening the policy and market case for plant-forward food reformulation.
insights.locator: Western diets—chock-full of fat, sugars, and red meat
